reasoning: The dead code finding is confirmed. In Metabox_Taxonomy::save(), line 97 performs `if ( ! isset( $_POST['auhfc'] ) ) { return; }`. Any execution that reaches line 129 has already passed this guard, which means $_POST['auhfc'] IS set at that point. The condition at line 129 (`if ( ! isset( $_POST['auhfc'] ) )`) is therefore always false and can never be true when reached — making lines 129-132 structurally unreachable dead code. The delete_term_meta() call on line 130 can never execute. There is no attacker-controlled path to the delete sink: (1) the early return at line 97 makes reaching line 129 with an unset auhfc key logically impossible; (2) even if somehow reached, nonce verification (lines 115-120) and capability check (lines 123-126) precede it, requiring an authenticated privileged user with a valid nonce. The finding is real as a code quality/logic error — the likely intent was to allow deletion by omitting the auhfc key, but the early-return guard at line 97 defeats this. There is no exploitable security issue; this is a missing feature or misapplied guard that results in dead code.
